data.title: BIZD In A Rate Cut Environment - Is Double-Digit Yield Sustainable?
data.description:
Introduction

I have a wide portfolio of stocks and instruments I follow and cover on this platform. As a young investor myself (I am under 30), I am very interested in tech, AI, EVs, and all kinds of things that make a lot of noise nowadays. But don’t be fast to close this article, because I’ve been breaking down different dividend picks to help my husband build a high-income dividend portfolio for himself, and it looks like I did a pretty decent job up until now, because he asked me to give him a few other ideas to add to his portfolio. It means the next few weeks I will be breaking down different instruments and sharing my findings here, with you.

I am sure some of you have been following me after my breakdown of Main Capital (MAIN), Capital Southwest (CSWC), or going beyond BDCs - FS Credit Opportunities (FSCO), JEPI (JEPI), Dynex Capital (DX), etc.

This new round of high-income dividend “hunting”, I want to start from the fund of BDC funds, VanEck BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:BIZD). There are a couple of reasons for that. First of all, my husband had missed Main Capital, even though I rated it as a buy, and it was a very timely rating, as the stock is up 25% in total since this breakdown. He missed it because Main gave about 7% return, but my husband is trying to sustain a 10-12% yearly yield, so he bought Capital Southwest instead. He was not interested in capital appreciation, only in dividend yield, which is okay.

The second reason to take a look at BDCs is the fact that it’s one of not many sectors that are actually hurt by lower rates. The Fed had already announced a .25 rate cut, and there is a possibility this is not the end of rate-cutting season. Today, I want to explain what BIZD is, who it is suitable for, and whether it is a good time to consider BDCs at all.

What Exactly Is BIZD and Who Is It For?

BIZD is a fund of BDCs. This is an ETF that owns a bunch of business development companies, which themselves are like a mix of private equity, venture capital, and a bank. They lend money to small and mid-sized businesses that can’t get a loan from other places for various reasons, and in return, BDCs earn high-interest income. By their structure, similar to REITs, BDCs are required to pass most of that income back to shareholders, which is why yields are so high.

Now, why not just buy one or two of the top names directly, like Ares Capital (ARCC) or Main Street Capital? You can, and I’ve covered both of them before. But for investors who don’t want to pick and choose, or who want broad exposure, BIZD could be the simplest solution. Instead of worrying whether you picked the “right” BDC, you get the entire sector, with all of the pluses and minuses of it.

BIZD will suit the income-focused investors who want double-digit yields (they could go back to single digits, beware) without managing each stock separately. If you enjoy picking them, following, and making decisions, you will be better off with individual instruments. It’s also a fit for people like my husband, who care less about capital appreciation and more about steady distributions. It’s less ideal for investors who are very sensitive to volatility, because BIZD can swing 20–30% in a year, or for those who believe they can pick the best BDCs themselves and outperform the average. Whether it’s a good time to look at BDCs in general, I will talk about later.

Yields

When you open BIZD’s yields, you see three different numbers. As of September 19, 2025, the distribution yield is 11.23%, the 12-month yield is 11.37%, and the 30-day SEC yield is only 8.73%.

The distribution yield (11.23%) is basically looking at the most recent dividend payment, and makes a simple extrapolation to the whole year by it. Meaning, they simply multiply it by four and annualize it. In simple words, it shows how much you’d make if the latest payout repeats every quarter. The 12-month yield (11.37%) is more backward-looking. It takes the actual dividends paid over the past year and divides that by today’s share price. This one tells you what the fund has delivered historically, based on its distributions.

The 30-day SEC yield (8.73%) is designed to show the income the portfolio is currently generating if you were to buy it today, standardized across all funds so investors can compare apples to apples. That’s why it often looks lower. In BIZD’s case, 8.73% is probably closer to the “true” earnings power of the underlying BDCs right now, while the higher 11% figures reflect what has been paid out in the past. It has various reasons, one of the simplest ones is an additional dividend (kind of a bonus) everyone gets because the rates are high and the BDCs are making more money than usual. I remind you, they must pay out a certain amount to the shareholders; they can’t keep this additional revenue only for themselves.

So, the 11%+ number is real, it’s what investors have been receiving up until now. But the 8.7% SEC yield is what you would have gotten, or will get if income generation cools off in a lower-rate environment, distributions could go down closer to that level. It doesn’t mean it will go there, but it could. But even at the lower SEC yield, you’re still looking at nearly 9% income, which is considered a high-yield.

Volatility and Past Performance

I want to start with volatility, because if you have a dividend portfolio and you like holding ETFs in it, most likely, you are more risk-sensitive than the average person. BIZD could get very volatile. In the past year alone, the ETF had a 12-month high of $17.81 (February 2025) and a low of $13.89 (April 2025). That’s a significant range and a reminder that this is not a safe, low-volatility income play.

Speaking of the low rates, the worst ever quarter of all BDCs was Q1 2025, when in March, rates were cut to about 0%. It was due to the pandemic, of course, and I can’t say for sure by how many percentage points lower rates hit their business, avoiding the pandemic sell-off, but in that month, BIZD plunged by 42.78%. Funny enough, the best-ever quarter was the one that followed, or Q2 2020, when it surged +34.34% in the recovery. This pattern shows the dual nature of BDCs: they fall very fast in downturns but rebound strongly when credit conditions stabilize.

Portfolio Holdings

BIZD holds 33 BDCs, which may sound very diverse, but the problem is that they don’t hold equal chunks. Ares Capital (ARCC) is by far the largest position, accounting for 16.6% of assets with about $273 million invested. Next is Blue Owl Capital (OBDC) at 10%, followed by Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) at 7.5%, and Main Street Capital (MAIN) at 5.5%. After that, you see smaller allocations to Hercules Capital, Golub Capital, Sixth Street Specialty Lending, FS KKR, and so on.

In total, the top five holdings make up more than 43% of the portfolio. So while this is technically a diversified basket of BDCs, you are very dependent on the largest players. The benefit is that these big names tend to be stronger, more diversified, and better capitalized. But if one of them performs weaker, the whole fund will be drawn down because of it.

Average Annual Returns

Since its inception in 2013, BIZD has delivered an annualized return of about 7.9%. Over the past decade, it’s closer to 9.8%, and in the last five years, the average jumps to 17.2%. Recent performance was due to the higher-rate environment and strong lending spreads in recent years.

Over the past year, BIZD returned 9.4% on NAV, and over three years, it averaged 12.4% annually. These are solid numbers for a high-yield fund, but it wasn’t the smooth ride. Once again, it has been volatile.

Distribution History

Consistency is a major strength of BDCs in general, and of BIZD as well. BIZD pays quarterly, and not like some of the BDCs that pay out monthly, but recent payouts were stable. They have been in the range between $0.42 and $0.46 per share. For example, in July 2025, the dividend was $0.4386. In April, it was slightly lower at $0.4295. In December 2024, it was higher at $0.4445, but the difference is really small.

Will it stay in the same range if the rates get low? Probably not. The dividend is tied to the performance, so there will be ups and downs if the performance weakens.

Fees and Expenses

I was surprised to see a total annual operating expense of 12.86%. That looks a lot, but then I learned that the actual management fee is only 0.40%, with another 0.02% in miscellaneous costs. The bulk of the expense, 12.44%, comes from something called Acquired Fund Fees and Expenses (AFFEs). Basically, it’s not the fees that you pay to VanEck, but the operating costs of the BDCs inside the fund. In simple words, BDCs already charge their own fees and expenses, which get passed to BIZD only for reporting. It’s an accounting issue, and I am not sure why it must be done this way.

What Happens When Rates Fall

Now the main question my husband was looking out for is what happens to BDCs, and BIZD in our case, if the Fed keeps cutting rates? The answer might look obvious because I mentioned many times until now that the BDCs are in a great place because of high rates, but in reality, it's more nuanced. BDCs earn less net investment income when short-term rates fall, because their loan portfolios are mostly floating-rate, while a good portion of their borrowing is fixed. That means this huge spread they had in the past two years will shrink. Their borrowing cost won’t change, but they will be getting less interest - income falls.

At the same time, lower rates make it easier for the businesses they lend to to pay back debt. Defaults go down, deal activity goes up, and BDCs can sometimes get equity gains from IPOs or acquisitions when markets are more open. In fact, the last time the Fed cut rates, BDCs held up surprisingly well and continued to pay out their distributions.

So while income may get lower from today’s highs, the overall business model of BDCs still works in a lower-rate environment. For investors willing to hold through cycles, the long-term picture is not as scary as it looks if you only focus on the falling spread.

Another thing worth noticing, when looking at total return levels, meaning the return investors get, including the dividend paid but not reinvested, BIZD, and many other BDCs have been outperforming the S&P500. After the cut, they may be in line or even underperforming the index. In fact, judging by the graph below, it looks like it could already be happening.

Conclusion

BIZD is one of the simplest ways to get exposure to the entire BDC sector. It saves you the work of picking winners and losers, but there is a downside as well. You’re heavily concentrated in a handful of big names. In return, you get quarterly distributions around 10–11%, exposure to some of the strongest BDCs on the market, and a product that has delivered nearly 8% annualized since inception in late 2013 despite multiple crises along the way.

Whether it is a good time to get exposure to the BDCs at all, it’s up to each of you. I, personally, would not build my entire portfolio around those right now, but at the same time, I see it as a solid instrument that could diversify a dividend-focused portfolio well. Even if the performance softens, the returns will still be decent enough.

data.title: Virtus Income & Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
General Market Environment

Equities, convertible securities, and high-yield bonds advanced in the second quarter on easing trade tensions, better-than-expected corporate earnings, and U.S. economic resiliency. The first quarter earnings season generally surpassed expectations, with top- and bottom-line growth rates exceeding consensus estimates, although many companies pulled or reduced full-year guidance on trade uncertainty.

Inflation measures subsided, employment data surpassed expectations, and real gross domestic product growth was estimated to resume in the second quarter, while consumer confidence fell. Key manufacturing and services surveys showed contraction. The U.S. Federal Reserve kept interest rates steady and continued to project two cuts by year-end. Against this backdrop, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield rose to 4.23%, but closed well below the intra-quarter high of 4.63%.

Equities Market Environment

  • The S&P 500® Index returned 10.94% for the quarter, bringing year-to-date performance to 6.20%.
  • Information technology, communication services, and industrials were the top-performing sectors in the quarter, while energy, health care, and real estate were the bottom-performing sectors.
  • Equity volatility reached 60.13 but finished lower at 16.73.

Convertible Securities Market Environment

  • The ICE BofA All U.S. Convertibles Index returned 8.88% for the quarter, bringing year-to-date performance to 6.55%.
  • Sector performance was mostly positive, with materials, industrials, and technology outperforming. Consumer staples, utilities, and energy underperformed.
  • Below-investment-grade (IG) issues outperformed IG issues. Equity-sensitive issues outperformed total return (balanced) and yield-oriented (busted) issues.
  • New issuance saw 44 issues priced, raising $32.5 billion in proceeds—the largest quarterly issuance in more than four years.

High-Yield Bond Market Environment

  • The ICE BofA U.S. High Yield Index returned 3.57% for the quarter, bringing year-to-date performance to 4.55%.
  • BB, B, and CCC rated bonds returned 3.46%, 3.47%, and 4.28%, respectively.
  • Spreads narrowed to 296 basis points (bps) from 355 bps, the average bond price rose to 97.12, and the market’s yield fell to 7.36%.
  • Industries were broadly higher for the period. Health care, media, and telecommunications outperformed. Energy, retail, and chemicals underperformed.
  • Trailing 12-month default rates finished the period at 1.41% (par) and 1.03% (issues).
  • Quarterly new issuance saw 87 issues priced, raising $77.3 billion in proceeds.

Portfolio Commentary

The portfolio benefited from strength across equities, convertibles, and high yield bonds.

Top contributors in the second quarter were driven by renewed optimism around the artificial intelligence build-out, following management guidance around capital expenditure and infrastructure spending during second quarter earnings season. Beneficiaries comprised several semiconductor companies, including Nvidia Corp. and Broadcom Inc., as well as hyperscalers such as Microsoft Corp., Meta Platforms Inc., and Amazon.com. A streaming platform operator and an enterprise software provider both rallied on strong beat-and-raise quarters, while another software holding with bitcoin exposure rallied alongside the cryptocurrency. Other top contributors to performance in the period were an aerospace manufacturer and a major bank.

Top detractors in the period included a health care provider that suspended guidance and underwent a key management departure. Regulatory headwinds impacted several portfolio holdings, with an executive order on drug prices pressuring pharmaceutical exposure, and legislative reform on utilities hampering a major gas and electricity provider. Apple Inc. reported solid earnings, but offered weaker-than-expected guidance on tariff uncertainty. Multiple payments companies were lower on point-of-sale results or acquisition-related headlines. Other top detractors in the period were an insurance provider, a satellite telecom company, and a food and beverage retailer.

Most option positions expired below strike and the portfolio was able to retain the set premiums.

Exposure increased in information technology and consumer staples, and decreased the most in consumer discretionary, financials, and communication services. Covered-call option positioning decreased quarter-over-quarter.

data.title: AAR Corp.: Strong Buy On Aerospace Aftermarket And MRO Growth (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
AAR Corporation (NYSE:AIR) delivered a strong Q1 2026 earnings beat, demonstrating the company’s momentum in the Maintenance, Repair, and Overhaul, or MRO, market. Since my last investor report, the stock has surged 27.6%, significantly outperforming the S&P 500’s 12.6% gain. In this article, I review the latest results, assess key risks and opportunities, and update my price target to support my continued buy rating on AAR Corporation.

AAR Sales Double-Digit Growth Is Not Enough For Wall Street

First quarter sales rose from $652.2 million to $739.6 million, beating analyst estimates by $50.2 million. Total sales were up 13% and 17% organically. The difference between absolute growth and organic growth is driven by the divestiture of the landing gear overhaul business in April 2025. Adjusted EBITDA increased 18% to $86.7 million, indicating a 40-basis point expansion in margins to 11.7%. Operating income grew 21% to $71.6 million, indicating margins of 9.7%, up from 9.1% a year earlier. Adjusted EPS grew 27% to $1.08 and beat analysts's expectations by $0.10. So, the company delivered a strong beat on the top and bottom lines.

The company reports its earnings through three segments:

  1. Parts Supply, which includes new parts and used serviceable materials.
  2. Repair & Engineering, which includes the company’s MRO activities.
  3. Integrated Solutions, which provides management and logistics support.

Part Supply sales grew 27% to $318 million, while adjusted EBITDA grew 34% to $43.8 million, indicating margin expansion from 13.1% to 13.8%. New parts distribution grew 20% across the commercial and government markets, while USM, or used serviceable material sales, ticked up. Repair & Engineering sales were down 1% to $214.6 million, driven by the divestiture of the Landing Gear business. Excluding this, the growth would have been 8%. Adjusted EBITDA grew 1% to $28.1 million, with margins expanding from 12.8% to 13.1%. Integrated solutions sales increased 10% to $185 million, driven by government and defense sales increases. Adjusted EBITDA grew 5% to $14.2 million, with margins sliding 30 basis points to 7.7%.

I previously noted that the divestiture of the landing gear business would bring the leverage down. The slide above, however, shows that sequentially this was not the case. The reason for that is that the company has invested in feedstock to fuel its USM business ahead. We do note that generally the net leverage has been improving.

What Are The Risks And Opportunities For AAR?

The main opportunities for AAR are continued demand for MRO and parts, both new and used. Currently, there still is a shortage of airplanes that requires airlines to keep existing airplanes in the fleet for a longer time, and those airplanes require maintenance. That fuels demand for parts and services. The drawback is that to get used serviceable materials, AAR has to buy and sell airplanes. Since the airplanes are now staying longer in service, the supply is somewhat limited, and that drives up costs. Simply speaking, it means the feedstock costs are higher, and AAR has to compete with other parties for those sales. So, the company has to assess case by case whether buying a certain airplane or engine makes sense to fuel their USM business.

Another opportunity is the increase of capacity. The company is working towards adding 15% capacity in 2026, which should drive upside for the business. A possible pressure could eventually become that as airplane supply from the original equipment manufacturers improves, the highly profitable aftermarket sales growth may taper. That could provide a short-term hiccup in growth, but for the long term, a growing global fleet will drive long-term growth in sales and earnings.

AIR Stock Upgraded To Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am upgrading AAR Corporation stock to a strong buy with a $102.04 price target, indicating 30% upside. This is driven by upward revisions of a couple of percent in the coming years for EBITDA and CAGR of around 9%. Free cash flow expectations have been revised downward, and as a result, net debt to EBITDA is expected to be 2x instead of 1.9x previously. However, we also see a credible path for the company to return to service its debt maturing in 2028, as free cash flow is still expected to grow substantially from prior levels. The 2029 (or 2028) maturities likely still need to be refinanced in part, but we see a path for the company to achieve its 1.4x EV/EBITDA as we also saw in FY23. Combining that with the upside and discount to the peer group, I believe that an upgrade to strong buy is justified.

Conclusion: AAR Corporation Stock Has Significant Upside

While free cash flow generation has been revised downward, I do believe that the company has a credible path towards reducing its leverage while the company should be able to grow its free cash flow substantially. So, I believe that the company is well positioned to service market demand and return value for shareholders, all while being significantly undervalued compared to aerospace and defense peers.

data.title: Midstream/MLP Free Cash Flow Yields Still Strong
data.description:
By Kyle Richards

For years, midstream companies have generated significant free cash flow (FCF), which has differentiated them from the broader market. With balance sheets in good shape, excess cash has been used to reward shareholders with dividend growth and opportunistic equity repurchases. Even with some capex creep related to a robust outlook for natural gas demand, midstream is expected to continue enjoying free cash flow tailwinds.

Midstream Stands Out for Strong FCF Generation

In contrast to the high-growth era of 2010–2020, when U.S. oil production more than doubled, midstream capital spending has generally moderated. This aligns with more measured oil and gas production growth in the U.S.

For years, broader energy has been focused on capital discipline, free cash flow generation, and returns to shareholders. As of September 17, energy had the highest trailing 12-month FCF yield of any sector in the S&P 500, per Bloomberg.

For many energy companies, FCF generation will fluctuate somewhat with commodity prices. Higher oil and gas prices would lead to more excess cash. However, midstream’s FCF generation tends to be more sustainable and predictable.

This is due to midstream’s fee-based business model backed by long-term contracts, which results in more stable cash flows. This is also why midstream companies can give year-ahead EBITDA guidance or multi-year expectations for EBITDA growth, in contrast to the rest of energy.

As shown below, midstream/MLPs have even higher trailing FCF yields than broader energy, represented by the Energy Select Sector Index (IXE). MLPs are represented by the Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index (AMZI).

Midstream is represented by the Alerian Midstream Energy Select Index (AMEI), which is ~75% U.S. and Canadian midstream corporations and ~25% MLPs. From a valuation standpoint, higher FCF yields for midstream/MLPs also can serve as an indication that this space has not become expensive on a relative basis.

Midstream MLPs and corporations have used excess cash to grow their dividends. Dividends are also generous relative to the broader market and IXE. As of September 17, AMZI and AMEI were yielding 7.6% and 5.4%, respectively. While midstream companies tend to prioritize dividend growth, companies have also deployed buybacks as a tool for returning excess cash to investors.

Free Cash Flow Tailwind to Remain Intact

Digging a bit deeper, the chart below shows FCF yields for some of the larger constituents of AMZI and AMEI. It uses 2026 consensus estimates from Bloomberg. Consistent with the index-level TTM FCF yields, MLPs tend to have higher 2026 FCF yields than their C-Corp counterparts.

This in part reflects last year’s strong gains for C-Corps with natural gas infrastructure, as well as healthy capital spending as these companies pursue natural gas pipeline projects. Even with solid gas-related project backlogs, free cash flow generation is expected to remain intact.

Notably, 10 of the 14 companies included in the chart are expected to have positive free cash flow after dividends. Four names are estimated to have more than $1 billion in excess cash after dividends, including Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Cheniere Energy (LNG), and ONEOK (OKE).

Pending transactions could cause some movement in 2026 FCF estimates for some names as analysts update their models. Names with notable deals underway include Plains All American (PAA/PAGP), Keyera (OTCPK:KEYUF), Sunoco (SUN), and Western Midstream (WES).

PAA/PAGP’s sale of its Canadian NGL business to KEY for $3.75 billion is expected to close in 1Q26. SUN agreed to acquire Parkland Corporation (OTCPK:PKIUF) in May for $9.1 billion, with closing anticipated in 2H25.

SUN expects the pro-forma company to generate over $1 billion of run-rate FCF after distributions (defined as adjusted distributable cash flow less distributions). Finally, WES recently announced the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions (ARIS) in a $1.5 billion deal expected to close in 4Q25. WES expects the deal to be accretive to 2026 FCF.

Why does FCF matter?

FCF enhances the overall financial flexibility of midstream MLPs and corporations. Excess cash can be used to further reduce debt or pursue bolt-on acquisitions.

With strong balance sheets in place, midstream corporations and MLPs often use excess cash for dividend growth and strategic buybacks. Midstream’s FCF yields stand out from the broader energy sector and equity market overall.

AMZI is the underlying index for the Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP) and the ETRACS Alerian MLP Infrastructure Index ETN Series B (MLPB). AMEI is the underlying index for the Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR) and the ALPS Alerian Energy Infrastructure Portfolio (ALEFX).

data.title: Kodak: KRIP Walking To The Bank
data.description:
Eastman Kodak Company (NYSE:KODK), the former photography giant, has faced a series of financial challenges, culminating this past month with a going concern warning. Fortunately for the company, management has been proactive. Last year KODK announced the termination of the Kodak Retirement Income Plan (KRIP), a defined benefit pension plan, to restock their coffers. The termination is expected to yield over $500m net proceeds to Kodak by the end of 2025. This should be enough to plug their balance sheet hole, buying time to focus on their new pharmaceutical endeavor and battery technology.

Background

KRIP is Kodak's heavily overfunded U.S. defined benefit pension plan, covering slightly under 2,000 current employees:

The initial plan was outlined as follows:

  • Selling $764.4m PE assets for $550.6m to Mastercard (MA) in Dec-24.
  • Selling $87.3m other PE assets for $61.7m to 4 other buyers (partially at 12/31/24 and $15.3m on 12/31/25).
  • Remaining “Illiquid Assets” have a net asset value of $161.3m as of Sep-24. KRIP also owned interests in hedge funds with a net asset value of $917.2m at Sep-24 for which they submitted redemption requests.
  • Kodak estimated KRIP would have surplus assets of between $885m and $975m after the satisfaction of KRIP’s liabilities.
  • To reduce excise tax from 50% to 20%, Kodak planned to transfer 25% of the surplus assets to a replacement plan for employees. After this and 20% excise tax, Kodak projects it would receive between $530m and $585m. Kodak projects no material income tax due to NOLs. In addition to the proceeds received by Kodak from KRIP, the Replacement Plan is projected to have assets having a value of between $220m and $245m which delays future contributions for a long time.
  • Kodak was previously required to use proceeds to repay their Term Loans until the balance is reduced to $300m and, thereafter, to use 50% of proceeds to prepay Term Loans until the amount of Term Loans is reduced to $200m, in each case plus a 1% prepayment fee. Kodak expected it would use approximately $315m of the Reversion Proceeds to satisfy these obligations, leaving a Term Loan balance of $200m and yielding interest cost savings of approximately $40m. Kodak projected that it would receive cash or other assets from the KRIP surplus having a value of between $215m and $270m.

The timeline has been updated through Q2-25 as follows:

  • Board approved the termination (Jan-25); benefits frozen Mar-25
  • Participant elections for benefit settlements (lump sums or annuities) closed Aug-25
  • Annuity payments are expected to be finalized in Oct-25
  • Full liabilities settlement and plan closure expected by Dec-25
  • $850m of excess assets are still expected after satisfying the KRIP obligations, which after funding the replacement plan and paying the 20% excise tax, Kodak expects to receive $500m ($300m cash/$200m illiquid assets).
  • Kodak now expects to use the first $300m to pay down the Term Loan in December and continue to pay it down with hedge fund redemptions through the maturity date in May-26, when they expect to have a balance approximating $175m.

The specific updated use of proceeds under the Term Loan are as follows:

On May 7, 2025, the Company and the Subsidiary Guarantors entered into the Second Amendment to the Amended and Restated Term Loan Credit Agreement (the “May 2025 Term Loan Credit Agreement Amendment”). The May 2025 Term Loan Credit Agreement Amendment provides the Company the option to pay the Cash Interest Payment entirely in PIK for the next six quarterly interest payments. In addition, the May 2025 Term Loan Credit Agreement Amendment revises the mandatory prepayment provisions under the Amended and Restated Term Loan Credit Agreement requiring Kodak to use 100% of the net cash proceeds from certain transactions to prepay Term Loans until the amount of the Term Loans is reduced to $200 million and, thereafter, to use 50% of the net cash proceeds to prepay Term Loans until the amount of the Term Loans is reduced to $100 million, in each case plus a 1% prepayment fee. The Company elected to pay the Cash Interest Payment for the second quarter of 2025 entirely in PIK.

Impact

Because the receipt of KRIP proceeds is not “probable” under GAAP, Kodak has been forced to include a going concern warning in their financials until this paydown is accomplished. Given the 10-Q disclosures, it does not appear Kodak will receive enough cash from the termination to fully pay off their term loan, so the balance will need to be rolled or paid off from additional capital raises. The $100m ATM put in place this summer seems to be with this possibility in mind, though it has yet to be tapped.

Kodak’s ongoing interest expense has been roughly the same as their year-to-date net loss, suggesting the company will be able to substantially reduce their cash burn after accomplishing this transaction. There also should be some positive earnings inflection from taking their pharmaceutical efforts from pre-production to a revenue generator.

Valuation

On a year-to-date basis, Kodak has earned $12m of operational EBITDA. The company does not provide guidance for the full year. Unfortunately, YTD results represent a 20% decrease from last year's run rate. Investors were certainly hoping for more after the ITC granted Kodak a sort of monopoly on U.S. printing plates last year, putting tariffs in place on foreign producers like FujiFilm that made import prohibitively expensive.

The company has not indicated how much they expect to earn from new initiatives the company is pursuing, but at a run-rate of $24m EBITDA (and declining), over a $600m market cap before debt, and some remaining preferred obligations, the stock does not appear cheap.

Other Considerations

Presumably as a sign of support for this process, the holder of the Series C Preferred converted their entire position into 15.1m common shares on August 8th, becoming KODK’s new largest shareholder. This was done at an effective price of $8.15/share, a significant premium and wiping out a $123m liability. About $100m of Series B preferred remains outstanding, however.

The focal point of Kodak’s pharmaceutical effort is as follows:

Compare this language to Donald Trump’s recent executive order mandating the stockpiling of key starting materials. Kodak appears to be positioning themselves to be a friendly partner to the administration's goals of reshoring manufacturing initiatives, specifically pharmaceuticals in this instance.

Kodak has been pushing other applications of their technology as well, recently spiking on the announcement that they have made some promising discoveries in the realm of battery technology. Time will tell if this is of significant commercial value.

Risks

Kodak's going concern risk will be mostly alleviated by completing their pension conversion; however, if that process were to hit a snag, they may be forced to take some drastic measures to avoid insolvency. Kodak does own significant real estate assets, primarily around their campus in Rochester, that could be used to collateralize new lending.

While new ITC tariffs were welcomed by Kodak's printing plate business, the benefit has not shown up as earnings continued to decline in the first half of 2025. I will be watching this business for signs of stabilization, without which it will be harder for Kodak to buy time to pursue their new initiatives.

Conclusion

Kodak's KRIP termination is throwing a life preserver to their balance sheet, allowing them to significantly cut down their expensive pile of debt. They got additional assistance from a key preferred stockholder converting at a large premium to the current stock price. Kodak appears to be betting on growth from pharmaceutical production to offset continued printing plate weakness and has made it to the finish line getting that facility up and running. While plenty of risk remains, Kodak could end up being a big winner from U.S. trade policy, and I will be following intently.

data.title: BGM Group: I Think I Found An Undervalued Stock
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Waihua Xu as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

BGM Group's (NASDAQ:BGM) strong financials, stable market share, and successful M&A track record suggest major upside. I rate the stock a Buy.

Investment Thesis

BGM started out as a traditional pharma company and has long led niche areas like heparin sodium and oxytetracycline in China. Its heparin sodium plant in Chongqing is the largest production base in the country, and the Sichuan-Chongqing region's big hog-farming and slaughtering industry makes raw material sourcing easy. For the four years prior, pharma revenue was steady at about $50M. It fell to $25M last year, mainly because the company proactively upgraded to more eco-friendly facilities. Even so, BGM kept solid financial stability and market share.

Since last December, after the new chairman, Chen Xin, who has a background in algorithms, took over, BGM has pivoted from traditional pharma into AI. Through all-stock deals, it quickly acquired several AI companies, bringing in advanced tech while keeping the core leadership and engineering teams. That broadened the business and strengthened its position in future growth areas.

In just six months, BGM's market cap jumped from under $50M to $2B - about 40x. Because of this shift, I treat BGM as a "new" stock. Even after the re-rating, the share price still trails many high-growth AI names. I think the market is still resetting how it sees and prices the company. With the AI businesses consolidated, the revenue mix and gross margin moved up, and free cash flow got more room to grow. But investors still compare it to low-growth, low-multiple API manufacturers and miss the higher margins and stronger operating leverage of the new lines. That's why I see potential undervaluation here.

Overlooked Inflection Point

Within just half a year, BGM acquired a series of targets that were already developing well on their own, including some in smart mobility and embodied robotics. In the most recently released financial report, although only the Insurance Business (AI insurance) and AI Solutions were consolidated, the AI business already accounted for 32.7% of revenue. At the same time, according to the report, first-half gross profit was +78% YoY, which implies that the AI business has higher gross margins and stronger profit elasticity.

Why is the market so sluggish? I guess it's because BGM has carried the "pharma company" label for so long. It's been a top player in China in the heparin sodium and oxytetracycline niches, so that drugmaker image is deeply ingrained. Meanwhile, China's insurance market is already dominated by AI-oriented giants like Ping An (OTCPK:PIAIF), Yuanbao (YB). So the market doesn't have much confidence in BGM stepping into AI insurance.

What I've learned, though, is that BGM's AI insurance model isn't trying to fight those giants head-on in the internet insurance "traffic war." Instead, it's building out an offline agent network, the A-side that's relatively overlooked by the big players, which actually lines up well with BGM's years of experience on the A-side.

As the series of acquired targets are consolidated and the AI business keeps producing, I believe BGM will sooner or later tear off the "pharma" label. Therefore, I plan to use a simple DCF model to estimate the per-share value and see whether this is an investment opportunity.

Valuation

I used the simplest DCF to calculate intrinsic value. For the discount rate, I first used CAPM, 10-year U.S. Treasury at 4.03%, for the market return, I used the Russell 2000's average return this year, 13.67%, for β I used 0.04 from TradingView (I also calculated β myself based on 170 trading days this year and it was only 0.05). This gives a cost of equity of about 4.42%. The company is basically net cash with little debt, I set the cost of debt at 1.2%, so WACC ≈ 4.41%.

For cash flow, based on information publicly available on the official website, disclosures, and common sense, I made my own rough estimates. Using the post-acquisition "baseline scenario," I set the profit & cash flow path for 2025-2028. For simplicity's sake, using a plain-vanilla DCF, I get an enterprise value of about $1.87B. With roughly $8.05 million in net cash, that puts equity value around $1.875 billion. On a basic share count of 98.295m, that's roughly $19.07 per share, versus the current $8.93 stock price, implying about +113% upside. This is consistent with my statement that it is undervalued.

Sensitivity is also straightforward, The reason the discount rate looks so low is simple: an ultra-low beta and basically no leverage. If you bump beta into a more typical range (say 0.5-0.8) or tack on 1-3 percentage points of size/idiosyncratic premium to CAPM, the discount rate moves from about 4.4% up to roughly 9-12%, and the DCF value comes down meaningfully. On the operating side, frame AI revenue mix/margins in high/ mid/low cases; if reality lands in the mid case, the valuation should sit between my base case and a conservative case. With more conservative discount and long-term growth assumptions, the upside narrows, but if AI execution beats that mid case, there's still room for the upside to open back up.

Risk

Although BGM's DCF suggests solid upside, there are several risks. First, the company is still in a phase where the market is relearning how to see and price it. Recognition of the AI business may take longer than expected, which could weigh on the share price. Second, rapid acquisitions and integration create execution risks across management, technology, and culture. The acquired companies are also spread across different locations, which makes integration harder. If integration falls short, revenue growth and profit leverage could suffer.

Additionally, the AI and AI insurance markets are highly competitive. Although BGM currently has an edge in offline agents on the A side, its ability to maintain overall competitiveness in the long run remains uncertain.

While BGM is relatively sound financially, fast expansion of AI operations and ongoing M&A could create capital pressure and near-term earnings volatility. If the new businesses fail to reach expected profitability, free cash flow flexibility could be affected, adding uncertainty to future results.

All in, if AI growth lags or integration remains shallow, my thesis may change. Investors should keep an eye on integration progress, financial performance, and how the market's view of BGM's AI business evolves.

Summary

In summary, consistent with my expectation of undervaluation, with β only 0.04-0.05 and an almost zero-leverage capital structure, a very low discount rate of CAPM ≈ 4.42% and WACC ≈ 4.41% is mathematically valid, and it also means weaker discounting of future cash flows and a higher intrinsic value. Accordingly, using a simple DCF, I estimate a fair value of about $19.07 per share. Compared with the current price of $8.93, there is about +113% upside, which supports the "undervalued" assessment. It needs to be emphasized that if β is raised to a more common range (0.5-0.8), or if 1-3 percentage points of size or specific risk premium are added on top of CAPM, the discount rate would rise roughly to 9%-12%, and the valuation would be clearly reduced; but as long as consolidation progresses and the AI business delivery is not weaker than the "medium" case, BGM's repricing and re-rating logic remains intact.

That said, it still needs to be noted that, as a company undergoing such a broad transformation, I still put a question mark on its integration capability, and a return of the valuation to normal may also have to wait until the acquired AI businesses continue to produce verifiable profit and cash flow on the financial statements. In addition, policy and regulatory changes, tougher industry competition, and the financial strain of rapid expansion could all add uncertainty to the company's future results. Given the above uncertainties, I tend to position it as a medium- to long-term allocation, and not suitable for short-term trading.

data.title: This Dividend Opportunity Is Disappearing Soon: Allete
data.description:
Co-authored by Treading Softly

Usually, at High Dividend Opportunities, we focus solely on great long-term income investments to hold for the long run. Today, we're taking time to unveil an opportunity that was previously only presented within our group.

Arbitrage is a trading methodology where you simultaneously buy and sell a holding between two markets where there's a pricing inefficiency – locking in a risk-free return instantly.

Other forms of short-term opportunities exist where a buyout is set to occur, but the market price is below the future buyout price. This can provide you with a simple, quick trade to lock in capital gains, and if the security pays a dividend, some nice income along the way.

Earlier this year, we highlighted such an opportunity to our community, and today we unveil it for everyone else - but this opportunity will not exist for much longer! In October, the buyout is set to be finalized.

Let's dive in.

Persistent Arbitrage Opportunity

Allete (NYSE:ALE), yielding 4.6%, is the parent company of a portfolio of energy and utility companies. The largest subsidiary of ALE's is Minnesota Power, a regulated utility company, and ALE's last hurdle before closing a buyout deal.

Last year, ALE reached an agreement to be acquired by the Canada Pension Plan Investment Board (CPP Investments) and Global Infrastructure Partners (GIP). CPP Investments has a long history of investing in U.S. companies, including regulated utilities. GIP is an infrastructure investment platform that was acquired by BlackRock (BLK) in 2024.

The acquisition plan includes acquiring all the outstanding common stock at $67/share. Since we added ALE to the Model Portfolio, we have already collected $2.19 in dividends. We stand to realize a capital gain if the deal is approved.

Currently, there is one final hurdle, and that is the Minnesota PUC (Public Utilities Commission). In Minnesota, the deal has faced some political pushback, with a particular focus on BlackRock.

The MPUC is currently scheduled to have a meeting on Sept. 25 at 9:00 AM Central, and an oral argument over this merger is the only issue on their agenda. Then, MPUC has a second meeting scheduled for Oct. 3 at 9:00 AM Central for a vote. Investors can find information on the meetings, agendas, and even links to webcast the proceedings live here.

Either of these meetings could have a material impact on ALE's trading price. At the oral arguments, we could see a response if it becomes clear that one side is favored to win the vote, and of course, the actual vote will have an immediate impact on ALE's share price. We currently expect the Commission's vote will happen at the Oct. 3 meeting. However, there's always a possibility that things will be extended.

If the merger is approved, then the closing process will start, and we can expect to receive $67.00 for our shares by the end of the year. The trading price would almost certainly trade up close to the $67 buyout price, allowing for those who wish to exit earlier for slightly less.

What happens if it isn't approved? Well then, ALE will continue as a public company. ALE's share price for the past year has been capped at $67. Even as many utilities have seen tailwinds from the prospects of declining interest rates and winning their rate cases to increase electric rates, ALE hasn't. In an attempt to garner support for the merger, ALE has agreed not to pursue a rate case. In the Q2 earnings release, Chief Financial Officer and Corporate Treasurer Jeff Scissons wrote:

"Absent the closing of the transaction and the rate case stay-out provision in the settlement agreement with the Minnesota Department of Commerce, Minnesota Power would be filing a rate case to account for reduced revenue, increased depreciation from capital investments and inflationary pressures." - Jeff Scissons, CFO

We expect the environment to improve for utilities going forward, and we're happy to increase our exposure to utilities. Regulated utilities have seen headwinds in the form of inflation and high interest rates, both of which are improving as rate cases are being approved across the nation and interest rates are slowly coming down.

If the merger is denied, we do expect that ALE's share price will fall sharply in the short term. There are a lot of arbitrage investors who are only in ALE for the merger, and as those investors look to exit, it will create downward pressure on the share price. We will not join them.

ALE has a long history of a stable and growing dividend. We like growing dividends, we want to own more utilities, and while ALE's dividend isn't particularly high compared to our portfolio, it is a generous yield for the sector. A sell-off because of a denial of the merger would be an opportunity.

If the buyout is approved, then we will have to live with a quick capital gain and possibly one more dividend. In short, we bought ALE because we're OK with either result.

Conclusion

We fully expect the buyout to be approved given the concessions made by ALE and its partners to complete it. If not, ALE is primed to enjoy the benefits of new rate cases (which are not occurring only as part of the buyout concessions) and the tailwinds pushing utilities higher. ALE is trading 4.6% below its buyout price of $67 at the time of writing, providing simple, quick returns for investors if the transaction is approved and closed.

If it doesn't, utilities are extremely reliable income investments that perform exceptionally well in falling-rate environments where there are also concerns of economic weakness. Sound familiar?

When it comes to retirement as an income investor, one's priority should be to replace their paycheck with dividend income. This replacement provides financial security and independence, making you self sustaining when so many others depend on the handouts and goodwill of others. It also means you're not depending on big tech not to stumble and falter, bringing down the market with it. Ride the waves with bountiful income. We can leverage opportunities like ALE as a low-risk way to see additional capital gains.

That's the beauty of my Income Method. That's the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Why The S&P 500 Could Be The Biggest Risk In Your Portfolio Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

When I started following the stock market at the age of 15 (in 2010), I followed the Dow Jones a lot. Back then, the index was a lot more popular, especially in the financial media, as quoting the Dow Jones’ daily point swings tends to make for much better headlines than doing the same for the S&P 500.

Right now, a 1% swing for the Dow Jones is a 460-point swing. For the S&P 500, that number is just 60 points. It doesn’t make for great headlines.

While the media is still quoting the Dow on very good and very bad days (i.e., DOW DROPS 1,000 POINTS), I don’t remember the last time I read a financial article/blog that discussed the Dow Jones Industrial Average. People don’t seem to care anymore.

Personally, I get that.

The Dow Jones, which was created by Charles Dow and Edward Jones in 1869, may be much older than the S&P 500, which was created in 1957, yet it has still become "irrelevant."

To use the findings of SmartAsset, there are a few major differences between the two indices that both enjoy a very strong name reputation:

  • The S&P 500 is a market-cap weighted index. This means bigger companies have a larger weighting. It makes sense. After all, why shouldn’t a large tech firm have a bigger weighting than a Pennsylvania-based steel producer? Especially if we assume the size difference is caused by a more successful stock price performance.
  • The S&P 500 covers 500 companies. The Dow Jones covers just 30. While we can make the case that 30 stocks are enough to diversify (see Meir Statman, 1987), having a diversified, market-cap-weighted basket of 500 stocks is still the safer way to go. Also, while I don’t have data to back this up, to me, it seems that successful companies are often added quite "late" to the Dow Jones. For example, as we can see below, as of May 29, 2024, Exxon Mobil (XOM) has massively outperformed Salesforce (CRM) since it replaced the oil giant in the Dow Jones. Needless to say, I am cherry-picking here and using somewhat old data. However, this is one of the risks of the index.
  • Speaking of diversification, the S&P 500 covers all major sectors. The Dow Jones, which has just 30 companies, does not.
  • On a very long-term basis, the S&P 500 has performed better than the Dow Jones, as the ratio between the two shows below. However, note that there have been decades when the Dow performed better.

That said, so far, I haven’t given you any groundbreaking info.

However, that may change, as the S&P 500 isn’t as attractive as it used to be.

As I have written in what feels like countless articles, its diversification benefits are largely gone. While it’s still a collection of 500 companies in 11 sectors, its largest ten holdings now account for nearly 40% of its weighting, the highest number in many decades, as we can see below.

Moreover, sector diversification is gone. Using Vanguard S&P 500 (NYSEARCA:VOO) data, we see that information technology accounts for 34% of its weighting. However, that’s not all, as the definition of "technology" has changed a bit. After all, Amazon (AMZN) is a consumer discretionary stock. That sector has an 11% weighting. Meanwhile, Alphabet (GOOGL) and Meta (META) are communication services companies, a sector with a 10% weighting.

Put together, these sectors have a weighting of roughly 55%.

As I have often said, it’s not a problem until it becomes a problem. It’s similar to a football team. Who cares if your team wins the championship just because it has 2-3 world-class players and a whole bunch of average players? Nobody.

However, it doesn’t make a team very sustainable, as it becomes dependent on a handful of players.

In sports, the New England Patriots may be a good example of this, as the team went downhill the moment quarterback Tom Brady left for Tampa Bay. ESPN dove into these developments last year to figure out what happened. Here’s a key paragraph from its introduction:

A slow erosion of high-end talent is at the root of the decline, combined with a rocky offensive coaching transition in 2022, and the near-impossible task of replacing an all-time great quarterback. - ESPN

The S&P 500 could be the Brady-era Patriots.

Even Goldman Sachs seems to agree, as my thesis is getting more and more support.

Hence, in this article, I’ll give you all the details, new developments, and explain where "smart money" is now putting its money, and what this could mean for the index.

So, as this intro has gotten long enough, let’s dive in!

Should We Look Beyond Big Tech?

I’m not the only one who has started to dislike the S&P 500 this year.

Goldman Sachs recently published a piece titled "Should Stock Investors Look Beyond the Tech Giants?" The timing of this article is quite fascinating, as large banks are usually late to the party. It does not happen very often that they publish a contrarian view while the party is still ongoing.

According to Goldman’s chief global equity strategist, investors will likely have to look beyond the one big group that currently dominates the market, as expected market returns could be low and stock-picking opportunities start to emerge.

Essentially, that’s my entire thesis in a nutshell.

Equity markets face headwinds that weren’t present during past structural bull markets. These include elevated valuations, higher interest rates and inflation, slower expansion of world trade, sluggish economic growth, and rising demands on government spending. Taken together, these factors mean that absolute returns are likely to be lower than during other sustained market rallies. - Goldman Sachs

See, another reason why I am bringing up this article is because it brings up my entire long-term thesis in the first two paragraphs, as it mentions structural factors like:

  • Higher for longer interest rates and inflation.
  • Global trade headwinds (deglobalization)
  • A bigger focus on government spending to drive growth (think the Inflation Reduction Act, pandemic relief, AI infrastructure spending, etc.)
  • Elevated valuations.

With regard to valuations, Goldman essentially makes the case that structural bull markets start with low valuations. This makes sense, as Crestmont Research states as well. The quote below is from a Crestmont Research paper from 2016. While it’s nearly a decade old, I still want to use it, as it’s very important:

Most notably, a secular bull cannot start from a level of high P/E - not only are the returns from earnings growth and dividend yield relatively low, but also there is little possibility of a positive contribution from P/E increasing when it starts at high levels. Secular bulls are periods with relatively high returns as the result of a positive addition from P/E increasing. - Crestmont Research (2016)

The chart below is also from 2016, yet it perfectly visualizes this effect:

Right now, we don’t have these tailwinds, as valuations are stretched. For example, one chart I often show is the one below, which tells us the market is trading at nearly 23.0x forward earnings, one of the highest numbers in decades, if we exclude the pandemic years.

Moreover, in credit, we are seeing the tightest high-yield spreads in at least three decades, which is a strong sign of a robust economy and willingness to take risks. After all, if investors, in general, were less willing to take risks, they would require much higher premia before putting any money in high-yield (read: junk) bonds. This reflects stock sentiment.

If I wanted, I could have gone with a super bearish title. Something like "Sell Everything Before It’s Too Late." However, while it may have gotten good views, it would have been very dishonest. See, as I have often said, this doesn’t warrant selling any equities.

Goldman agrees, as it makes the case that these numbers simply reflect strong fundamentals and a strong expected surge in margins. For example, if AI really becomes the disruptive force I expect it to become, we can assume most S&P 500 companies will be able to substantially boost their margins. That would justify an elevated global market cap to global GDP ratio, as we can see below.

The higher the margins, the more efficiently revenues/GDP can be used to create shareholder value. To put it differently, there is no reason to compare current valuations with valuations of 20 years ago.

Needless to say, while the Big Tech giants are still in a good spot to grow, there are reasons to distrust the risk/reward. On top of everything I already mentioned, we are dealing with risks like "winner takes all" in AI. Unlike ten years ago, the large hyperscalers are increasingly competing with each other, including in cloud computing, AI models, and AI solutions. This somewhat erodes their competitive moats and forces them to aggressively spend on CapEx to lower disruption risks.

As much as I like companies like Amazon and Alphabet, in general, the risk/reward for these companies has deteriorated a bit.

Another problem is that we don’t get support from bonds. While longer-term bond yields have come down, they remain persistently high. In prior cycles, lower rates have fueled sustained stock market rallies, as lower bond yields make equities relatively more attractive.

Here’s Bloomberg’s take on this issue, which covers major topics like government finances and inflation. I entirely agree with the quote below:

The rising yields signal investors are demanding extra compensation for holding government debt in the face of spiraling budget deficits and sticky inflation. The mounting worry is that politicians lack the ambition, or even the ability, to rein in their countries’ debt, while central banks may struggle to combat the mix of sustained price pressures and ebbing economic growth. - Bloomberg

So, now what?

Diversification, Not Reinventing the Wheel

On top of not being bearish, I’m also not telling anyone to change their entire portfolios.

For example, in our family, most accounts that mostly rely on ETFs still keep the S&P 500 as a core holding. The only difference is that more future cash is flowing into other ETFs with a high market correlation, yet other benefits. We’re also buying more single stocks, as we believe this is the perfect environment to use single stocks for risk/reward improvements.

If you ask Goldman Sachs where to diversify, it would tell you to pick companies that have competitive advantages against high-end Chinese manufacturing competition. That’s an increasingly important thing to keep in mind, as the Chinese have gone from buying high-quality manufactured goods from nations like Germany and the U.S. to boosting their own exports.

Earlier this year, the New York Times wrote an article titled "The Tsunami Is Coming": China’s Global Exports Are Just Getting Started." While Chinese manufacturing may seem a bit off-topic in this article, it’s one of the factors investors need to be aware of when investing in a world where globalization rules seem to be rewritten, for lack of a better description.

Moreover, higher tariffs, a weakening dollar, and more fiscal support (+ localization) are factors that all favor American companies with a domestic focus and dominant positions in their markets. Essentially, buy American companies with wide-moat benefits and limited geopolitical risks.

To give you an idea of what companies would come into play here, let me provide some food for thought:

  • Companies with a domestic consumer focus are Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST). While neither is cheap, I expect a business model like the one Costco has used to grow to result in long-term benefits.
  • Domestic manufacturing benefits companies like Caterpillar (CAT), Cummins (CMI), and Deere & Company (DE). Although these companies all have tariff risks due to cross-border manufacturing and sales, they should do well if domestic cyclical growth improves and North American trade tensions ease.
  • While I am not a fan of the cyclical nature of steel companies, I believe Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), Nucor (NUE), Steel Dynamics (STLD), and others will be big winners in the next upswing.
  • Defense companies remain on top of my buy list. Especially companies that are hard to disrupt. This includes GE Aerospace (GE), RTX Corp. (RTX), L3Harris (LHX), and Northrop Grumman (NOC).

Then there’s tech.

Despite all the things we have discussed, technology companies are not a no-go area.

We just need to be prepared for broadening growth.

“The success of the technology sector as a driver of positive returns for investors is likely to continue, albeit with a broadening in investment opportunities,” Oppenheimer writes. Software as a service (Saas) companies may be able to use AI tools to create productivity gains for enterprises, for example. And a sharp increase in AI infrastructure may lower the barrier to entry for new companies, cannibalize some existing business models, or lower prospective returns on their increased capital expenditures. - Goldman Sachs

Are you ready for a mind-blowing chart?

This is the comparison of American tech earnings compared to world (ex-U.S.) earnings:

I also believe U.S. tech companies will remain on top of their international peers. On top of having better access to cheap power to fuel their data centers (think of dirt-cheap gas in the Permian and Marcellus basins) and more favorable regulations (a lot of government support!), American tech companies spend way more on CapEx and R&D than their European peers. And, as someone who lives in Europe and follows politics daily, I don’t expect that to change anytime soon.

All of this favors AI infrastructure players like Arista Networks (ANET), Comfort Systems USA (FIX), EMCOR Group (EME), CoreWeave (CRWV), and many others that I discussed in a recent in-depth article. And, as I briefly mentioned, I believe Amazon is one of the companies in a terrific spot to use its massive installed base (think of Prime) to scale AI applications.

Another company I haven’t given the attention it deserves is Micron (MU), which combines both AI benefits and cyclical operations through sales to automotive buyers and others.

In general, Goldman makes the case that investors should use a "barbell" approach by investing in both fast-growing tech companies and the companies that facilitate growth. In general, the barbell approach is a great way to balance safety and growth, which is why I will soon put way more emphasis on this strategy.

For now, I would like to refer you to yet another one of my articles, as I could not agree more with Goldman. That’s why I wrote, "Oh Boy, The Entire Stock Market Hinges On Just 1% Of Stocks."

In that article, I brought up energy stocks like natural gas producers, midstream companies with higher yields that ship energy from Point A to Point B (like Kinder Morgan (KMI)), as well as diversified companies like LandBridge (LB).

Horizon Kinetics, which was featured in that article, explained that because so much capital has flown into Big Tech, a rotation to smaller beneficiaries could trigger massive alpha due to a lot of capital chasing a few smaller opportunities.

While this rotation could take time, I fully agree with that assessment.

It also brings me to the takeaway.

Takeaway

While the S&P 500 remains a core holding of many family accounts, I believe it’s time to rotate, as the index reminds me a lot of the Patriots when they still had Tom Brady.

Various factors, like valuation headwinds, concentration risks, sticky inflation, and unfavorable bond yields, have created a situation that could pave the way for subdued long-term returns.

However, instead of making the case that we should avoid stocks or even sell them. I think secular growth, like AI tailwinds and the potential of broadening economic growth, favors equities. We just need to diversify.

That’s where wide-moat domestic players come in, including industrials, defense contractors, energy stocks, high-yield midstream players, infrastructure giants, and even tech companies that have what it takes to expand their moats.

That’s also why I will continue to provide actionable ideas in the weeks and months ahead.

For now, my main message is to diversify. The S&P 500 is still my favorite index, but I doubt it’s possible to generate favorable returns without making at least some portfolio changes.

data.title: American Assets Trust: Raised Guidance, But Headwinds And Dividend Sustainability Warrant Caution (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

The REIT (XLRE) sector is expected to see tailwinds from lower base rates in the coming months. As a result, these should provide relief, as REITs typically borrow debt to grow.

With the Fed lowering rates 50 basis points last year, some have already enjoyed success, seeing their prices rise as a result. With one rate cut last week and more cuts expected by the end of 2025, I expect REITs could potentially outperform over the next year.

However, those with exposure to the office space, like American Assets Trust (NYSE:AAT), still face headwinds as the sector has struggled to recover from COVID. And AAT's latest quarter showed headwinds still lingered, despite them managing to raise full-year guidance.

In this article, I discuss American Assets Trust's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Thesis

I last covered AAT back in February in an article titled: Now May Be The Time To Back Up The Truck On This Near 6% Yield.

At the time, I considered the REIT's near 6% yield, solid fundamentals, and upside potential to their price target of $26 attractive for long-term investors.

Management's confidence in office utilization increases, promise in their multi-family segment's growth, and well-staggered debt maturities were also contributing factors to my buy rating.

However, I did mention that the expected decline in their Office segment could result in downside. Since then, the stock is down an additional 5.55% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up over 7% over the same period.

Mixed Performance

American Assets Trust reported their Q2 earnings at the end of July with somewhat mixed results. FFO of $0.52 was flat from the prior quarter, and same-store NOI was also flat. Year-to-date NOI increased 1.4% compared to the previous year's quarter.

Including their lease termination fees of $800,000, FFO actually declined a penny, missing analysts' estimates by $0.02. Revenue of $107.93 million also missed by a modest $0.3 million.

As a result of lease termination fees, revenue also declined from $108.61 million. However, their Q1 miss was larger, missing estimates by $3.44 million.

AAT's performance and a mixed operating environment were obvious signs of lingering headwinds. They completed 102,000 square feet of leasing activity in Q2. Comparable rent spreads, despite increasing 10% on a straight-line basis, fell 2% on a cash basis, a result of backfilling a 12,000-square-foot property.

However, management stated they have solid momentum going in the next quarter with 17,000 square feet of executed leases and 111,000 square feet of LOIs.

Segment Performance

In terms of their segments, NOI was flat for all (segments), down from growth of 2.1% the prior year. Their retail segment performed the best, with same-store cash NOI growth of 4.5%.

They executed 220,000 square feet of renewals and new leases, seeing higher spreads of 7% on a cash basis and 22% on a straight-line basis. The retail portfolio was 98% leased, up from 95% the prior year.

Their multifamily segment portfolio was in line, impacted by student move-outs, and one of their properties in Portland's occupancy dipped below 85% due to seasonal turnover.

As a result, NOI declined 3.9% due to lower rental income and higher expenses, down significantly from Q2'24, where the portfolio's NOI growth was 9.5%.

As previously mentioned, their office segment saw comparable rent spreads fall 2%. The office portfolio was 82% leased, down from 86.6% due to an expected move-out. Management stated part of this space has already been backfilled.

Their mixed-use portfolio's impact was the most obvious, a clear sign that consumers are facing financial distress. NOI in the segment declined 5%, impacted by lower traffic at Embassy Suites in Oahu. Last year's quarter saw positive NOI growth of 2.2%.

This year's quarter was impacted by lower rental revenue, softer consumer & domestic leisure demand, and elevated labor costs & expenses. Management mentioned they remained confident in the property's long-term positioning. And I suspect demand is likely to pick up in the next 6 to 12 months as base rates come down gradually.

Raised Guidance

Despite their mixed results, AAT managed to raise full-year FFO guidance by a penny with a midpoint of $1.95. FFO is expected in a range of $1.89 to $2.01, up from $1.87 on the low end. However, at midpoint, this still represents a 24.4% decline from 2024's $2.58.

And while this does sufficiently cover their full-year dividend payout of $1.36 on a per-share basis, their funds available for distribution, similar to AFFO, declined year-over-year from approximately $34.8 million to $27.35 million. With $26.29 million in dividends paid, this put the payout ratio dangerously high at 96.1%.

Going forward, investors should keep a close eye out for increased leasing activity, particularly in their office segment, as well as FAD growth. Further declines in the coming quarters could force management to reduce the dividend.

Investment-Rated Balance Sheet

On a positive note, American Assets Trust's balance sheet was solid with plenty of available liquidity and well-staggered debt maturities. They had $144 million in cash and $400 million in revolver capacity.

Additionally, they are investment-grade rated from all 3 major agencies and have no debt maturing until 2027, which does give them some flexibility regarding their dividend safety.

Leverage was a little higher than I preferred at 6.3x, although office REITs usually operate at higher leverage ranges than some REIT counterparts.

For comparison, this was lower than both CTO Realty Growth (CTO) & Global Net Lease (GNL), whose net debt to EBITDAs were 6.9x and 6.6x, respectively. AAT's management stated they were targeting a range of 5.5x or lower in the future.

Valuation and 6.5% Yield Are Attractive

With a forward multiple of 10.66x and a dividend yield of 6.5%, American Assets Trust could be particularly attractive. However, despite lower base rates providing potential tailwinds, macro uncertainty and their elevated payout ratio keep me cautious.

For investors looking to own because of their low valuation, I would wait to see sequential improvements in their financials and segments, as further economic weakening could result in further downside.

Conclusion

American Assets Trust's mixed performance in their latest quarter shows headwinds will likely continue to plague the REIT, at least for the near to medium term.

Although likely to see tailwinds from lower base rates as this will provide relief for consumers, positively impacting their mixed-use segment, office leasing uncertainty remains a risk. Going forward, investors should continue to keep a close eye on both as well as growth in AAT's bottom line.

Despite the attractive multiple and 6.5% dividend yield, macro uncertainty and dividend sustainability are the main two reasons for my downgrade from buy to hold.

data.title: Virtus KAR Small-Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Broad U.S. equity markets experienced significant volatility in the second quarter, largely driven by the burgeoning trade war. Markets dropped over 10% in early April following the U.S. “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, then surged to new highs by late June as tariff concerns eased among investors. A momentum-driven headwind prevailed, driving lower-quality, higher-volatility stocks to generally outperform higher-quality, lower-volatility stocks—penalizing the kinds of stocks KAR focuses on, which offer the potential for growth with a smoother ride over the long term.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Small-Cap Value Fund (MUTF:PXQSX) returned 3.81% (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell 2000® Value Index, which returned 4.97%. An underweight in the information technology sector, combined with stock selection in the financials sector, detracted from performance. Stock selection and an overweight in the consumer discretionary sector contributed positively to performance, as did an underweight in the energy sector.

Construction Partners (ROAD) and The Cheesecake Factory (CAKE) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Civil infrastructure company Construction Partners reported significant revenue growth and margin expansion. Results were driven by good operational execution in a strong road infrastructure spending environment, combined with meaningful acquisition activity.
  • Restaurant chain The Cheesecake Factory is benefiting from resilient comparable-store sales growth relative to the broader casual dining industry, as well as moderating food and labor inflation. Also, the company continues to expand its North Italia and Flower Child branded restaurants, which we believe are opening at attractive economics.

Watsco (WSO) and Hillman Solutions (HLMN) were the largest detractors from performance during the quarter.

  • Watsco is a leading distributor of heating, ventilation, air conditioning, and refrigeration (HVAC/R) equipment. The company is facing revenue headwinds from weaker equipment sales, particularly in its commercial business, due to recent refrigeration transition disruptions. In addition, the residential market is beginning to show signs of softness heading into the peak cooling season.
  • Hillman Solutions is a leading provider of hardware solutions and home improvement products, serving retailers, contractors, and consumers. The company’s underperformance stems from its exposure to foreign-made goods and the impact of tariffs on its cost structure.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, there were no new purchases for the portfolio, and we sold Azenta, Lakeland Financial (LKFN), and Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG).

  • Our investment in life sciences company Azenta resulted from our position in the predecessor company, Brooks Automation, and was based on its highly specialized cold storage business. While Azenta’s core business of cold storage unit sales and storage services remained solid, a sizeable acquisition of another cold storage company serving emerging markets had a material negative impact on the company’s financial results and business predictability. While the other company has since been divested and there has been a change in senior management, our concerns over the company’s capital allocation discipline remained. While we continue to believe that Azenta’s differentiated capabilities in robotic automation and gene synthesis are still intact, we did not have sufficient confidence in the new management team to overcome our concerns over the elevated execution risk associated with the ongoing business transformation.
  • We reduced our position in Lakeland Financial in late 2024 when the shares reacted favorably to the results of the U.S. presidential election. However, our previous concerns over the bank holding company’s internal controls and underwriting discipline persisted, reducing our confidence in the management team. With these considerations in mind, we sold our remaining position.
  • We sold our shares in Scotts Miracle-Gro due to the lawn and garden care company’s lack of consistent capital allocation discipline. We became particularly concerned when Scotts announced the unexpected departure of its CFO. In view of the company’s large family ownership/board representation, we believe Scotts needs a strong, independent voice that the current chairman/CEO (the son of the company’s founder) does not provide. It became clear that the chief accounting officer serving as interim CFO was to be given the permanent CFO role, and this ruined our hopes for a strong replacement.
